title: Ring of Fire: Tribal Sovereignty & Governance
subtitle: Pacific Rim — A 40,000 km Arc of Living Nations
core_concept: The Sovereignty Stack: Governance, Territory, Resources, Data, and Infrastructure are five vertically nested layers. No infrastructure project along the Ring of Fire is viable unless it engages all five layers — beginning at the top.
through_line: The Ring of Fire was, before colonization, a Ring of Governance. Building infrastructure that ignores sovereign nations is not a network — it is a pipeline drawn through other people's living rooms.

A.name: Territorial Rights and Legal Frameworks
A.content: UNDRIP, FPIC, ILO Convention 169, national implementation comparison

B.name: Nation-by-Nation Governance Profiles
B.content: Salish Sea Nations, Ontario Ring of Fire First Nations, Ainu of Hokkaido, Maori of Aotearoa

C.name: Resource Sovereignty and Critical Minerals
C.content: James Bay Lowlands, Pacific geothermal, fisheries and food sovereignty

D.name: Cooperative Infrastructure Models
D.content: KNET, Wasaya Airways, Marten Falls equity, Lummi Cherry Point, Karuk cultural fire

E.name: Indigenous Data and Digital Sovereignty
E.content: CARE Principles, OCAP, Maori Data Sovereignty, Center for Tribal Digital Sovereignty

safety_critical.rule: Sovereignty is inherent, not granted. Never frame Indigenous rights as concessions from settler governments.
FPIC is a right, not a process. Never reduce it to a consultation checkbox.
Use each nation's preferred name and governance terminology.
Respect data sovereignty — do not aggregate or publish data that nations have not made public.
Acknowledge disputed territories without taking political positions.

relationship_to_wmk: This mission extends the sovereignty framework from WMK Module 6 to the entire Pacific Rim. Wilma Mankiller's Bell water project model — communities building their own infrastructure — is the philosophical foundation for the cooperative infrastructure models in Module D.
